title: Topological filters: a toolbox for processing dynamic signals
speaker: Michael Robinson
abstract: Using the geometry of sheaves as the common language, this talk will bridge three separate areas: dynamical systems, signal processing, and data fusion. Because sheaves model consistency relationships between local data, they are easily assembled from detailed models of systems. Being topological in nature, sheaves mediate local-to-global inference. By incorporating local geometry from the start, the global "fit" between local data and models can be quantified, which supports robust inferences about missing or inaccurate data.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Sheaf-theoretic signal-processing thesis
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames sheaf theory as a practical way to encode physical data-acquisition systems, compare data with a model via a consistency radius, and regard data fusion and filtering as optimization and morphism-like operations.

proof_stages.label: Finite-space sheaf models for sensor systems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker restricts attention to computable finite Alexandrov spaces arising from preorders or partial orders, assigns local data spaces as stalks, and uses commuting restriction maps to express consistency of local data.

proof_stages.label: Assignments, global sections, and consistency radius
proof_stages.summary: Observed data are formalized as assignments to stalks; global sections are perfectly consistent assignments, and the consistency radius measures all restriction-map discrepancies and bounds distance to the nearest global section.

proof_stages.label: Data fusion as consistency-radius minimization
proof_stages.summary: Minimizing consistency radius is presented as a practical data-fusion procedure that can improve estimates, quantify data/model mismatch, and identify which sensors or restriction maps are responsible for large inconsistency.

proof_stages.label: Classical filters as sheaf morphisms
proof_stages.summary: Discrete linear translation-invariant filters are recast using input, output, and internal-state sheaves, where the internal sheaf encodes a shift register and the wiring maps reproduce the usual hardware/filter implementation.

proof_stages.label: Quasi-periodic topological low-pass filter construction
proof_stages.summary: The new filter is built in two stages: first a learning or factorization step estimates a quasi-periodic base topology for the signal, and then sheaf morphisms average data over that learned topology rather than over ordinary contiguous windows.

proof_stages.label: Performance and application evidence for topological filters
proof_stages.summary: The quasi-periodic low-pass filter is compared with standard filters and applied to chirps, radar imagery, and musical time series to show amplitude stability, denoising, and anomaly-detection capabilities.

proof_stages.label: Implementation agenda and limitations
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by emphasizing prototype software, the need for probabilistic/statistical analysis, and computational bottlenecks where advances from topological data analysis may help.

examples.name: Camera overlaps and the Čech complex
examples.note: Overlapping camera views are used to make concrete how a sensor deployment can be converted into a finite topological space, with or without a hole depending on common overlap.

examples.name: Noisy sinusoid parameter estimation
examples.note: Estimating amplitudes, phases, and frequencies of a sum of sinusoids demonstrates how minimizing consistency radius can outperform standard signal-processing algorithms at low signal-to-noise ratio, albeit more slowly.
examples.engagement_reason: he encodes noisy sinusoid parameter estimation as a sheaf optimization, minimizes RMS consistency radius, and compares algorithmic errors versus signal-to-noise ratio

examples.name: Segundo missing and extra drum hits
examples.note: Abrupt shifts in an eleven-note percussion pattern motivate using the quasi-periodic low-pass output as a baseline and subtracting it to build a high-pass anomaly detector for missing or duplicated notes.
examples.engagement_reason: he processes the Segundo rhythm with the quasi-periodic filter and a high-pass residual, thresholds anomalies, and distinguishes missing from duplicated drum hits via the ringing pattern
